title: Invacare Perfecto2 Oxygen Concentrator - Low Oxygen Purity Alarm
description: Troubleshooting low oxygen purity alarms caused by airflow restriction, filter issues, cannula problems, environmental conditions, or internal concentrator failure.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm whether the oxygen concentrator is actively supporting a patient. If the patient requires continuous oxygen therapy, have clinical staff move the patient to another verified oxygen source before troubleshooting. Expected outcome: The patient is not dependent on a concentrator with questionable oxygen output. If patient safety is restored, continue troubleshooting only in a safe area.

steps.title: Confirm the Reported Alarm
steps.instructions: Power the Invacare Perfecto2 on and allow it to run long enough to confirm the reported low oxygen purity alarm. Observe the alarm behavior, indicator lights, audible alarm, and whether the alarm occurs immediately or after warm-up. Expected outcome: The issue is confirmed before unnecessary parts replacement or repair escalation.

steps.title: Check the Oxygen Flow Setting
steps.instructions: Verify the flowmeter is set within the prescribed and manufacturer-supported operating range. Confirm the flow ball is stable and not set above the device’s rated output. Expected outcome: Excessive flow demand is ruled out as a cause of low oxygen concentration. If reducing the flow to an approved range clears the alarm and oxygen purity stabilizes, document the finding and stop.

steps.title: Inspect the Cannula, Tubing, and Humidifier Bottle
steps.instructions: Check for kinked tubing, crushed cannula lines, blocked nasal cannula tips, excessive tubing length, or a clogged humidifier bottle. If a humidifier bottle is installed, verify it is not overfilled, cracked, cross-threaded, or creating back pressure. Expected outcome: External patient accessory restrictions are corrected. If replacing the tubing, cannula, or humidifier bottle resolves the alarm, return the unit only after performance is verified.

steps.title: Check the Outlet Port and Oxygen Connection
steps.instructions: Inspect the oxygen outlet fitting for obstruction, damage, looseness, or residue. Confirm the tubing is fully seated and not leaking at the outlet connection. Expected outcome: Oxygen flow is leaving the concentrator without obvious restriction or leakage.

steps.title: Inspect the External Cabinet Filter
steps.instructions: Check the external cabinet filter for dust, lint, pet hair, blockage, or moisture. Clean or replace the filter according to facility procedure. Expected outcome: Adequate room air intake is restored. If the alarm clears after filter service and oxygen output verifies within acceptable limits, document the correction and stop.

steps.title: Check the Room Environment
steps.instructions: Verify the concentrator has adequate clearance around the cabinet and is not placed against a wall, curtain, bed linen, or furniture. Confirm the unit is not operating in an excessively hot, dusty, or poorly ventilated area. Expected outcome: The concentrator has enough airflow for cooling and sieve bed operation. Why it matters: Restricted intake or poor cooling can reduce concentrator performance and trigger purity alarms.

steps.title: Listen for Abnormal Compressor Operation
steps.instructions: With the device running, listen for unusual compressor sounds, weak operation, loud rattling, rapid cycling, or air leaks. Do not open the unit for internal diagnosis during basic troubleshooting. Expected outcome: Obvious signs of internal compressor, valve, or pneumatic failure are identified for repair escalation.

steps.title: Check for Obvious External Damage
steps.instructions: Inspect the cabinet, power cord, caster area, outlet fitting, and filter cover for damage from impact, transport, liquid exposure, or contamination. Expected outcome: Physical damage is identified before the unit is returned to service. If damage is found, remove the concentrator from service.

steps.title: Verify Output With Appropriate Test Equipment
steps.instructions: In a Biomed-controlled setting, connect an oxygen analyzer and verify oxygen concentration after the unit has warmed up. Confirm the analyzer is calibrated or verified before relying on the reading. Expected outcome: The low oxygen purity complaint is either confirmed or ruled out using an independent measurement. If oxygen concentration remains low after external checks, stop troubleshooting and escalate for repair.

CCR.Complaint: Clinical staff reported the Invacare Perfecto2 was alarming for low oxygen purity during patient oxygen setup.
CCR.Cause: External inspection found a heavily clogged cabinet filter and restricted airflow around the concentrator.
CCR.Resolution: Cleaned/replaced the cabinet filter, restored proper clearance, verified stable flow and acceptable oxygen concentration, and returned the unit to service.

helpfulDetails: Whether the alarm occurred immediately or after warm-up
Flowmeter setting at time of complaint
Tubing, cannula, and humidifier bottle condition
Any kinks, leaks, or blocked accessories found
Alarm light and audible alarm behavior
Signs of heat, dust, odor, or cabinet damage
Final device status: returned to service or removed for repair

title: Invacare Perfecto2 Oxygen Concentrator - No Flow Or Low Output Flow
description: Troubleshooting no oxygen flow or low outlet flow caused by power, tubing, cannula, humidifier, filter, or external restriction issues.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions:
If the concentrator is in patient use and no flow or low flow is reported, have clinical staff place the patient on an alternate oxygen source immediately.

Expected outcome: The patient is supported by a known-good oxygen source before troubleshooting begins.

If alternate oxygen is needed, stop troubleshooting at the bedside until the patient is safe.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Flow Problem
steps.instructions:
Turn the Invacare Perfecto2 on and allow it to run briefly in a safe, non-patient-care area.

Check whether the issue is:

No flow at the outlet
Low flow compared with the flowmeter setting
Flowmeter ball not rising
Flow present at the unit but not at the cannula
Intermittent flow

Expected outcome: The actual symptom is confirmed before parts or accessories are replaced.

steps.title: Check Power and Startup Condition
steps.instructions:
Confirm the unit powers on normally and does not shut down, alarm, or run unusually weak.

Verify the power cord is fully seated, the outlet is functional, and the concentrator is not connected through a questionable extension cord or power strip.

Expected outcome: The concentrator starts and runs with normal motor sound and indicator behavior.

If the unit will not power on or immediately alarms, troubleshoot the power/startup issue separately.

steps.title: Check the Flowmeter Setting
steps.instructions:
Confirm the flow control knob is not fully closed or set below the requested flow rate.

Slowly adjust the flowmeter while observing the flowmeter ball.

Expected outcome: The flowmeter ball rises smoothly and can be adjusted to the prescribed flow range.

If the ball is stuck, erratic, or does not respond, the issue may involve the flowmeter or internal pneumatic path.

steps.title: Remove Patient Tubing and Test Flow at the Concentrator Outlet
steps.instructions:
Disconnect the cannula, extension tubing, and humidifier bottle if installed.

Check for flow directly at the oxygen outlet using an appropriate flow test method.

Expected outcome: Flow is present at the concentrator outlet.

If flow returns after removing accessories, the concentrator may be functional and the restriction is likely external.

steps.title: Inspect Cannula and Oxygen Tubing
steps.instructions:
Check the cannula and tubing for kinks, crushing, moisture buildup, clogged prongs, or excessive tubing length.

Swap with known-good oxygen tubing and cannula.

Expected outcome: Flow improves with known-good tubing.

If this resolves the issue, document the tubing or cannula restriction and return the concentrator to service only after confirming proper output.

steps.title: Check the Humidifier Bottle, If Used
steps.instructions:
Inspect the humidifier bottle for cross-threading, blocked diffuser, overfilling, cracked cap, loose seal, or incorrect assembly.

Remove the humidifier bottle and connect tubing directly to the concentrator outlet for testing.

Expected outcome: Flow improves when the humidifier is bypassed if the bottle was causing restriction or leakage.

If the humidifier is defective, replace it and retest.

steps.title: Inspect External Filters and Air Intake Area
steps.instructions:
Check the cabinet air filter and intake area for dust, lint, blockage, or placement too close to walls, bedding, curtains, or furniture.

Clean or replace external filters according to facility procedure.

Expected outcome: Air intake is clear and the unit can breathe properly.

A restricted intake can reduce concentrator performance and may contribute to low flow or alarms.

steps.title: Check for Outlet or Fitting Damage
steps.instructions:
Inspect the oxygen outlet fitting for looseness, cracks, broken threads, missing adapter, or signs of damage from tubing being pulled.

Expected outcome: The outlet is secure and not leaking.

If the outlet fitting is damaged, remove the device from service for repair.

steps.title: Listen for Abnormal Sounds or Leaks
steps.instructions:
With the unit running, listen for hissing, weak compressor operation, cycling irregularity, rattling, or air escaping from inside the cabinet.

Do not open the device for internal diagnosis during basic field troubleshooting.

Expected outcome: The concentrator runs smoothly without obvious leak sounds or unusual mechanical noise.

If abnormal sounds are present, internal pneumatic or compressor evaluation is likely needed.

steps.title: Verify Output With a Flow Analyzer or Calibrated Flowmeter
steps.instructions:
Connect appropriate test equipment and compare actual output flow to the flowmeter setting.

Expected outcome: Delivered flow matches the selected setting within acceptable facility limits.

If the indicated setting and measured output do not match, remove the device from service for bench evaluation.

steps.title: Check for Heat, Smell, or Signs of Damage
steps.instructions:
Inspect the cabinet, cord, plug, vents, and surrounding area for unusual heat, odor, discoloration, liquid intrusion, or physical damage.

Expected outcome: No unsafe condition is present.

If heat, smell, electrical damage, or liquid intrusion is found, stop testing and label the unit Out of Service.

CCR.Complaint: Clinical staff reported the Invacare Perfecto2 had little to no oxygen flow at the patient cannula.
CCR.Cause: Found the humidifier bottle cap cross-threaded, causing leakage and reduced delivered flow.
CCR.Resolution: Reinstalled a known-good humidifier bottle, verified outlet flow with test equipment, and returned the concentrator to service.

helpfulDetails: Final device status: returned to service or removed from service

title: Invacare Perfecto2 Oxygen Concentrator - Compressor Overheating Or Thermal Shutdown
description: Troubleshooting Perfecto2 compressor overheating, hot operation, intermittent shutdown, airflow restriction, and cooling-related external causes.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: If the Perfecto2 is in active patient use and shuts down, overheats, alarms, or produces unreliable oxygen flow, remove it from patient use immediately. Transfer the patient to a backup oxygen source according to facility policy. Expected outcome: The patient is not dependent on an oxygen concentrator with unstable compressor operation.

steps.title: Confirm the Reported Symptom
steps.instructions: Power the unit in a controlled Biomed area and verify the complaint. Check whether the issue is: Unit shuts down after running for a period of time; Compressor becomes unusually hot; Thermal protection trips; Reduced airflow or low oxygen alarm occurs before shutdown; Cabinet is excessively warm; Fan is not running or airflow is weak. Expected outcome: The overheating complaint is confirmed before parts or repair decisions are made.

steps.title: Check for Proper Placement and Ventilation
steps.instructions: Verify the concentrator is not pushed against a wall, bed, curtain, cabinet, or other obstruction. Confirm the air intake and exhaust areas have adequate clearance. Expected outcome: Air can move freely around the cabinet. If poor placement was causing heat buildup, reposition the unit, run it under observation, and stop if normal operation returns.

steps.title: Inspect the External Cabinet and Air Openings
steps.instructions: Look for blocked vents, lint accumulation, dust, pet hair, plastic bags, labels, or bedding material restricting airflow. Clean external debris using approved facility cleaning methods. Expected outcome: Cooling air paths are visibly clear. Restricted airflow is a common external cause of compressor overheating.

steps.title: Check the Intake Filter Condition
steps.instructions: Inspect the cabinet intake filter or external filter area for dust loading, clogging, moisture, or improper installation. Clean or replace the filter according to facility practice and manufacturer-compatible parts policy. Expected outcome: The concentrator can pull in adequate room air. If a clogged filter caused the issue, run the unit and verify stable operation.

steps.title: Verify the Cooling Fan Operation
steps.instructions: Power the unit on and observe whether the cooling fan runs normally. Listen for abnormal fan noise, scraping, intermittent operation, or no fan operation. Expected outcome: The fan runs consistently and moves air through the unit. If the fan does not run, runs intermittently, or has weak airflow, remove the unit from service for repair evaluation.

steps.title: Check Room Temperature and Environment
steps.instructions: Confirm the unit is not operating in a hot room, near a heater, in direct sunlight, inside a closet, or in a poorly ventilated area. Expected outcome: The unit is operating in a reasonable clinical environment with normal room ventilation. Excess ambient heat can cause compressor temperature to rise even when the concentrator itself is not internally failed.

steps.title: Inspect the Power Cord and Outlet
steps.instructions: Check the power cord for damage, loose plug fit, heat discoloration, or signs of arcing. Test the outlet with an approved electrical safety or outlet tester if appropriate. Expected outcome: The unit is receiving stable AC power without evidence of cord or outlet overheating. If the outlet or cord is suspect, stop testing and correct the power source issue before further evaluation.

steps.title: Check for Abnormal Compressor Sound or Vibration
steps.instructions: While running the unit, listen for loud knocking, buzzing, grinding, excessive vibration, or compressor strain. Expected outcome: Compressor sound is steady and consistent. Abnormal sound combined with overheating may indicate compressor wear, restriction, or internal mechanical load.

steps.title: Verify Output Flow Setting
steps.instructions: Confirm the flowmeter is set within the rated operating range for the Perfecto2. Make sure the ball is stable and not bouncing unusually. Expected outcome: Flow is set appropriately and remains stable. Running outside normal flow expectations can contribute to performance complaints or alarm conditions.

steps.title: Check Patient Tubing and External Accessories
steps.instructions: Inspect tubing, humidifier bottle, cannula, and connectors for kinks, blockage, water accumulation, crushed tubing, or incorrect attachment. Temporarily test with known-good tubing and no humidifier bottle if facility procedure allows. Expected outcome: External accessories are not restricting output flow. If the concentrator runs normally after removing or replacing a restricted accessory, document the accessory issue and return the unit only after performance is verified.

steps.title: Check for Humidifier Bottle Restriction
steps.instructions: If a humidifier bottle is used, inspect for overfilling, clogged diffuser, incorrect bottle type, cracked cap, or cross-threaded connection. Expected outcome: The humidifier does not create excessive back pressure or flow restriction. A blocked humidifier can make the concentrator work harder and may contribute to heat or alarm complaints.

steps.title: Run the Unit Under Observation
steps.instructions: After correcting external issues, run the concentrator at a normal clinical flow setting in a ventilated Biomed area. Monitor for: Stable operation; Normal fan airflow; No thermal shutdown; No unusual heat; No burning smell; No abnormal compressor noise; Stable flow. Expected outcome: The concentrator operates without overheating or shutting down. If the issue does not recur and all checks pass, document findings and return according to facility policy.

steps.title: Check Oxygen Output Performance if Available
steps.instructions: Use an approved oxygen analyzer after the unit has warmed up and stabilized. Expected outcome: Oxygen concentration is within acceptable facility/manufacturer expectations. If oxygen concentration is low, unstable, or alarm behavior continues, remove the unit from service for further evaluation.

steps.title: Do Not Continue Repeated Thermal Testing
steps.instructions: If the unit repeatedly overheats, shuts down, smells hot, or trips thermal protection, stop testing. Expected outcome: The device is not stressed further and is not returned to patient use. Repeated overheating may damage internal components or create a reliability risk.

CCR.Complaint: Clinical staff reported the Invacare Perfecto2 shut down after extended operation and the cabinet felt unusually hot.
CCR.Cause: Biomed found the intake filter heavily dust-loaded and the unit positioned with restricted rear cabinet ventilation.
CCR.Resolution: Cleaned external vents, replaced the intake filter, repositioned the unit for proper airflow, ran the concentrator under observation, and verified stable operation without thermal shutdown.

helpfulDetails: Whether the unit was in active patient use
Flow setting at time of shutdown

title: Invacare Perfecto2 Oxygen Concentrator - Dirty Or Restricted Cabinet / Intake Filters
description: Troubleshooting poor airflow, overheating, alarms, or reduced performance caused by dirty or restricted external cabinet and intake filters.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: If the Perfecto2 is in use on a patient and an alarm, low oxygen concern, or airflow issue is reported, have clinical staff move the patient to an alternate oxygen source before troubleshooting. Expected outcome: The patient is supported by a verified oxygen source before the concentrator is inspected. If patient therapy is restored with another device, continue troubleshooting only when the concentrator is safe to remove from service.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Issue
steps.instructions: Confirm the complaint with the reporting staff. Ask whether the unit had: Reduced flow output, Low oxygen alarm, Excessive noise, Overheating, Poor cooling airflow, Visible dust buildup, Frequent filter-related service concerns. Expected outcome: The issue is confirmed and tied to airflow restriction, contamination, or poor cooling.

steps.title: Inspect the Exterior Cabinet
steps.instructions: Look over the cabinet vents, rear intake area, side panels, and lower cabinet openings. Check for dust mats, lint, hair, tape, plastic bags, bedding, or wall obstruction blocking airflow. Expected outcome: Cabinet vents are open and unobstructed. If a visible obstruction is found, remove it, clean the exterior, retest the unit, and stop if normal operation returns.

steps.title: Check Unit Placement
steps.instructions: Verify the concentrator is not pushed tightly against a wall, curtain, bed, or furniture. Oxygen concentrators need open airflow for cooling and compressor operation. Expected outcome: The unit has adequate clearance around air intake and exhaust areas. If poor placement caused the restriction, reposition the unit, run the device, and confirm normal operation.

steps.title: Inspect the Cabinet Filter
steps.instructions: Remove and inspect the external cabinet filter according to facility procedure. Look for: Heavy dust buildup, Lint, Hair, Discoloration, Moisture, Physical damage, Filter material breakdown. Expected outcome: The filter is clean, dry, intact, and seated correctly. If the filter is dirty, clean or replace it per facility policy. If the issue resolves after filter service, document the corrective action and stop.

steps.title: Check for Missing or Incorrect Filter Installation
steps.instructions: Confirm the filter is installed in the correct location and fully seated. A missing, incorrectly seated, or wrong filter may allow contamination into the cabinet or restrict airflow. Expected outcome: The correct filter is installed properly. If the filter was missing or incorrect, replace it with the correct approved filter and inspect for excessive internal dust contamination through visible openings only.

steps.title: Inspect Intake Path for External Restriction
steps.instructions: Without opening the concentrator beyond normal user-serviceable access, inspect the intake area for lint, dust mats, labels, or debris. Expected outcome: Intake path is visually clear from the outside. If debris is removable from the outside, clean it carefully and retest the unit.

steps.title: Power On and Observe Operation
steps.instructions: Plug the Perfecto2 into a known-good outlet and turn it on. Observe for: Normal startup, Cooling fan operation, Abnormal noise, Alarm condition, Airflow restriction, Unusual heat, Burning smell. Expected outcome: The unit starts normally without airflow-related alarms or overheating. If the device operates normally after filter cleaning, return it to service only after performance checks required by facility policy are completed.

steps.title: Verify Flow Output Setting and Delivery Path
steps.instructions: Check that the flowmeter is set appropriately for testing and that tubing or accessories are not kinked or blocked. A filter issue may be reported as low output, but tubing, humidifier bottles, or cannulas can also restrict flow. Expected outcome: Flow path is open and the flowmeter responds normally. If an accessory restriction is found, replace the accessory and retest.

steps.title: Check for Recurring Alarm or Overheating After Cleaning
steps.instructions: Allow the concentrator to run long enough to confirm the alarm does not return. Monitor cabinet temperature, airflow noise, and any visual or audible alarm behavior. Expected outcome: Unit runs normally without recurring airflow, temperature, or oxygen-related alarms. If alarms return after filters and external restrictions are addressed, remove the unit from service.

CCR.Complaint: Nursing reported the Invacare Perfecto2 was alarming and appeared dusty around the cabinet intake area.
CCR.Cause: External cabinet filter was heavily restricted with lint and dust, reducing cooling airflow.
CCR.Resolution: Removed unit from service, cleaned/replaced cabinet filter, cleared exterior intake area, verified normal startup and operation, and returned unit to service after required checks.

helpfulDetails: Alarm behavior before and after cleaning

title: Invacare Perfecto2 Oxygen Concentrator - Sieve Bed, Valve, Or Pressure System Fault
description: Troubleshooting low oxygen concentration, pressure alarms, abnormal cycling, or suspected sieve bed, valve, compressor, or pressure system problems.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the patient from the Invacare Perfecto2 if oxygen delivery is questionable, alarms are active, or flow cannot be verified. Place the patient on another approved oxygen source according to facility or clinical procedure. Expected outcome: Patient oxygen therapy is maintained on a known-good source. Why it matters: A concentrator with pressure or sieve bed issues may produce inadequate oxygen concentration even if flow appears present.

steps.title: Confirm the Reported Symptom
steps.instructions: Power the concentrator on in a controlled area and observe the complaint. Look for: Low oxygen concentration alarm; Pressure alarm; No flow or weak flow; Abnormal compressor cycling; Continuous alarm after startup; Oxygen purity below expected range; Unusual exhaust or purge sound. Expected outcome: The reported issue is confirmed before deeper troubleshooting begins. If the device operates normally during observation, document the conditions tested and return only if performance checks are acceptable.

steps.title: Check the Power Source and Startup Behavior
steps.instructions: Verify the unit is connected to a known-good outlet. Inspect the power cord for damage, loose strain relief, overheating, or intermittent connection. Power the unit on and listen for compressor startup. Expected outcome: The concentrator powers on normally, the compressor runs, and alarms clear after the normal startup period. If the unit does not power on or the compressor does not start, troubleshoot as a power or compressor startup issue instead.

steps.title: Inspect the Cabinet, Air Intake, and Filters
steps.instructions: Check that the concentrator has adequate clearance around the cabinet. Inspect the external cabinet filter, intake area, and vents for lint, dust, blockage, or signs of restricted airflow. Clean or replace external filters per facility procedure. Expected outcome: Airflow paths are clear and the unit is not overheating or starving the compressor for intake air. If cleaning the filter restores normal pressure and oxygen concentration, verify operation and stop.

steps.title: Verify Flowmeter Setting and Outlet Flow
steps.instructions: Connect a known-good flow test device or flowmeter as appropriate. Set the Invacare Perfecto2 to the prescribed or test flow range. Confirm that the flowmeter ball is stable and that flow is present at the outlet. Expected outcome: Outlet flow is stable and matches the selected setting. If flow is weak, unstable, or absent, continue checking tubing, humidifier components, and internal pressure behavior.

steps.title: Check for External Restrictions or Leaks
steps.instructions: Remove any patient tubing, cannula, humidifier bottle, or accessory connected to the outlet. Test the concentrator output directly with known-good test tubing. Inspect the humidifier bottle, outlet fitting, oxygen tubing, and connector for cracks, loose fit, cross-threading, or blockage. Expected outcome: External accessories do not restrict flow or leak oxygen. If removing an accessory restores normal operation, replace the faulty accessory, verify output, and stop.

steps.title: Listen for Normal Pressure Cycling
steps.instructions: With the unit running, listen for the compressor and sieve bed valve cycling. Normal operation should include repeating pressure/purge cycling sounds. Watch for: No valve cycling; Rapid cycling; Weak or muffled purge; Loud exhaust burst; Compressor laboring; Alarm occurring at the same point in the cycle. Expected outcome: The concentrator cycles consistently without abnormal pressure buildup or loss. Why it matters: Sieve bed and valve faults often show up as abnormal cycling, pressure imbalance, or poor purge behavior.

steps.title: Check for Obvious Tubing or Fitting Problems
steps.instructions: Remove the unit from service before opening any access panels allowed by facility policy. Inspect accessible tubing, fittings, and connections for: Loose tubing; Kinked tubing; Cracked tubing; Disconnected pressure lines; Moisture contamination; Damaged fittings. Expected outcome: Accessible pneumatic connections are secure and unobstructed. If a loose external or accessible connection is corrected, perform a full operational check before returning the device to service.

steps.title: Verify Oxygen Concentration With a Calibrated Analyzer
steps.instructions: Allow the concentrator to warm up according to facility test practice. Measure oxygen concentration using a calibrated oxygen analyzer at the outlet. Test at the normal clinical flow range used by the facility. Expected outcome: Oxygen concentration remains within acceptable performance limits for the device and facility standard. If oxygen concentration is low despite proper flow, clear filters, and no external restrictions, suspect sieve bed, valve, compressor, or pressure regulator/system fault.

steps.title: Evaluate Pressure System Behavior Without Performing Deep Repair
steps.instructions: If facility procedure and test equipment allow, perform non-invasive pressure verification at approved test points only. Look for: Low system pressure; Pressure not building; Pressure bleeding down too quickly; Pressure imbalance between sieve beds; Relief valve activity; Compressor unable to maintain pressure. Expected outcome: Pressure system behavior supports normal sieve bed cycling and oxygen production. If pressure readings are abnormal, do not continue clinical use.

steps.title: Check for Heat, Odor, or Mechanical Stress
steps.instructions: While the unit is running, observe for unusual heat, burning smell, vibration, rattling, or compressor strain. Expected outcome: The concentrator runs without overheating, odor, or abnormal mechanical noise. If overheating, burning smell, or severe compressor noise is present, power the unit down, remove it from service, and escalate for repair.

steps.title: Determine Whether the Fault Is External or Internal
steps.instructions: If the following have been ruled out: Bad outlet or power issue; Blocked filter or restricted airflow; Faulty tubing or humidifier accessory; Incorrect flow setting; Obvious accessible tubing issue. and the concentrator still has low oxygen concentration, pressure alarms, abnormal cycling, or unstable output, the likely issue is internal. Possible internal causes include sieve bed degradation, valve failure, compressor weakness, pressure regulator issue, leaking pneumatic component, or pressure sensor/control fault. Expected outcome: The device is not returned to service until repaired and performance verified.

CCR.Complaint: Clinical staff reported the Invacare Perfecto2 alarmed during use and appeared to deliver weak oxygen flow.
CCR.Cause: External tubing and humidifier bottle tested good, filters were clear, but the unit produced low oxygen concentration with abnormal pressure cycling during bench evaluation.
CCR.Resolution: Removed the concentrator from service, labeled it Out of Service, and sent it for repair due to suspected sieve bed, valve, or internal pressure system fault.

helpfulDetails: Any unusual heat, vibration, odor, or noise
